g.data-narration: When no free frame is available and a page fault occurs, a page replacement algorithm selects a victim page to evict. Goal is to minimize the number of page faults.
g.text: Selecting which page to evict when memory is full

g.data-narration: FIFO replacement: replace the oldest page in memory. Simple to implement using a queue. Suffers from Belady's anomaly where more frames can cause more faults.
g.text: Belady's anomaly: more frames = more faults!

g.data-narration: Optimal replacement: replace page that will not be used for longest period in future. Gives lowest fault rate. Impossible to implement since we cannot predict the future. Used as benchmark.
g.text: Replace page not used for longest time

g.data-narration: LRU replacement: replace page least recently used. Good approximation of optimal. Uses past behavior to predict future. Requires hardware support for timestamps or stack.
g.text: Needs HW: counter or stack per page

g.data-narration: LRU approximation: reference bit set by hardware on access. Second-chance algorithm: FIFO plus check reference bit. If bit set, give second chance and clear bit. Clock algorithm.
g.text: Reference bit: set on access by HW
Bit=1: clear and skip | Bit=0: replace

g.data-narration: Enhanced second-chance: use reference bit and modify bit pair. Four classes from zero-zero to one-one. Replace from lowest class first. Reduces IO by preferring clean pages.
g.text: Prefer clean pages to reduce disk writes

g.data-narration: Counting-based algorithms: LFU replaces page with smallest count. MFU replaces most frequently used. Neither commonly used as they do not approximate OPT well.
Page replacement algorithms minimize faults. OPT is theoretical best. LRU is practical best. Second-chance clock algorithm is most commonly implemented in real operating systems.
